does: This paper develops a quantitative model for evaluating and tracking dangerous AI capabilities over time, establishing a framework to assess when systems cross specified danger thresholds. The model operationalizes how capability testing translates into risk estimates and enables policy decisions conditional on danger levels. Through simulations, the authors demonstrate two primary failure modes in dangerous capability detection: systematic bias in danger estimates and temporal lag in identifying threshold crossings. They characterize how these failures arise from two key sources: uncertainty in the dynamics of AI capability development and competitive pressures among frontier labs that may incentivize faster deployment or testing shortcuts. The work provides concrete mechanisms through which policy makers can reason about the adequacy of their monitoring systems and the conditions under which they may fail to detect emerging risks in time.
connects: This work builds on a growing literature in AI safety that emphasizes the importance of systematic evaluation and early detection of dangerous capabilities, extending frameworks from both computer security and high-stakes systems monitoring. It responds to earlier conceptual work on responsible AI development and capability-targeted evaluation by providing a formal mathematical treatment of how detection systems perform over time. The paper connects to broader research on AI governance and policy mechanisms that condition intervention on observable system properties, drawing analogies from regulated industries where threshold-based monitoring guides regulatory action. It also engages with work on capability forecasting and AI timelines by treating capability dynamics as a parameter to be reasoned about, and addresses longstanding concerns about racing dynamics and race-to-the-bottom incentives in competitive AI development.
enables: This framework creates infrastructure for the AI safety and policy communities to rigorously assess dangerous capability evaluation programs, supporting decisions about resource allocation, testing frequency, and appropriate institutional structures. The model enables comparative analysis of different monitoring strategies and their failure modes, pointing toward optimization of capability evaluation protocols. It establishes foundations for understanding how uncertainty in capability dynamics and competitive structure affect warning signal reliability, opening avenues for research on robust detection under both epistemic and strategic uncertainty. The work sets up future investigation into how policy mechanisms—such as licensing requirements, third-party auditing, or information sharing between labs—might reduce bias and lag in detection systems, and supports development of more sophisticated game-theoretic models of capability evaluation under competition.
